beeFacts: Bees have existed for more than 100 million years.
Honey bees have 170 odorant receptors and have a sense of smell 50 times more powerful than a dog.
Bees have 2 stomachs – one for eating, and one for storing nectar and processing it into honey.
Honey bees' antennae have more than 300 taste receptors.
Bees have five eyes.
Honey bee brains are about the size of a sesame seed.
A pollen basket, or corbicula, is the structure on the outer hind leg of worker honey bees that holds the pollen.
Honey bees cannot see red light but can see ultraviolet light, enabling them to see intricate ultraviolet patterns on flower petals that humans cannot see.
Honey bee eggs hatch after three days and develop into larvae. Queens mature in 16 days, workers in 21, and drones in 24.
Worker bees are 10–15mm at maturity, drones 15–17mm, and queens 18–20mm — nearly twice the size of workers.
Worker bees live two to four weeks in summer, but winter bees, called diutinus, can live up to 11 months.
Drones live four to eight weeks and die soon after mating; queens typically live two to three years.
Both worker bees and queens have stingers; drones are stingless.
Honey bees can taste with their feet, with claws especially attuned to salt.
Honey bee eyes are textured with the same hexagonal pattern as honeycomb.
Because of their acute sense of smell, bees can be trained to detect explosives, bombs, and landmines.
Honey bees' fuzzy bodies are a disadvantage in wet conditions — water droplets adhere to the fuzz, adding weight and requiring more energy to fly.
On a single foraging trip, a worker bee will visit between 50 and 100 flowers.
A single bee can carry up to 35% of their body weight in pollen.
When a bee finds a food source, it communicates its location to others through the 'waggle dance,' a figure-eight movement conveying direction, distance, and size.
During hot weather, worker bees cluster on the outside of the hive to keep cool — a phenomenon called 'bee bearding.'
Bees navigate using the sun as a compass and can find their way even on cloudy days by detecting polarized light.
When temperatures drop below around 50°F, bees form a ball to conserve heat, maintaining 90–100°F at the center near the queen.
Bees communicate within the hive by releasing pheromones, passing them on through grooming and feeding.
The queen releases a distinct pheromone called Queen Mandibular Pheromone (QMP).
Every bee colony has its own distinct scent so members can identify each other.
Honey bees do sleep, with foragers sleeping more deeply at night and house bees napping in short bursts across 24 hours.
Cumulatively, bees sleep five to eight hours per day, broken into as many as 40 to 50 naps.
Worker bees cycle through hive jobs as they age — from cleaning and nursing to comb-building, guarding, and finally foraging.
Honey bees do not store much water in the hive; specialized foragers collect it on an as-needed basis.
The buzzing of honey bees tends to be in the key of C.
When bees fly, the rapid flapping of their wings builds up a positive static electric charge that attracts negatively charged pollen grains.
Bees maintain a temperature of 92–93°F in their central brood nest regardless of outside conditions.
Older honey bees can effectively reverse brain aging when they revert to nursing — a role typically handled by young bees.
Honey bees use their wings to cool their hives by fanning air through the entrance.
Studies have shown that caffeine improves honey bees' memory and learning capabilities.
Queen bees are created by being consistently fed royal jelly throughout the entire larval period, while worker and drone larvae only receive it for three days.
When a queen dies, workers raise multiple new queens; upon hatching, they fight until one kills the others.
Queens mate with 10 to 40 drones per mating flight, then never mate again — yet their eggs remain fertilized for the rest of their lives.
Queens can lay between 2,000 and 3,000 eggs per day, averaging one egg every 43 seconds in their prime.
A queen bee's stinger is smooth, unlike a worker's barbed stinger, allowing her to sting multiple times.
If a queen is removed from the hive, the loss of her pheromones alerts the colony within 15 minutes.
When a new queen emerges, she begins 'piping' — a tooting sound that prompts caged rival queens to respond with duck-like quacking pulses.
Worker bees are 75% genetically related to each other, compared to 50% for human siblings.
When foraging, worker bees can fly up to 20 miles per hour by beating their wings 200 times per second.
During a typical foraging day, worker bees make around 30 trips within a two-mile radius of the hive.
Drones develop from unfertilized eggs and have no father — only a mother and a grandfather.
Drones have larger eyes on the top of their heads to help them identify queens during mating.
During mating season, as many as 25,000 drones from dozens of hives gather in a drone congregation area to mate with queens.
Swarming bees can generate 100 to 1,000 volts per meter of electrostatic charge — similar to a thunderstorm.
